problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem C (data) on Asian giant ("sparrow") hornet sightings in Washington State. Teams were given a WSDA dataset of public reports (with Lab Status: Positive ID / Negative ID / Unverified / Unprocessed), reporter notes, and image files. The five sub-problems: (a) predict the hornet's spread over time and assess precision; (b) predict the likelihood of mistaken classification using the dataset and images; (c) prioritize which reports to investigate as most likely positive; (d) explain how to update the model with new reports and find the optimal reporting/update frequency; (e) define what would constitute evidence of eradication in Washington State. The core challenge is extreme class imbalance (only 14 positive vs ~2000+ negative reports) plus heterogeneous data types (geo-coordinates, free-text notes, photos).
models_used: Factor analysis / PCA-based environmental-suitability model (Model 1): 16 abiotic variables -> z-scores -> first 4 principal components (87.9% variance) weighted by variance share into an ES score ES=500+100*Y_total
K-means clustering (Model 2, spatial): per-month cluster centers of confirmed sightings, with the number of centers preset per month from the hornet colony cycle (0 in winter up to 6 in Jul/Aug)
Gaussian Mixture Model (Model 2, spatial): mixture of Gaussians centered on K-means cluster centers, weights pi_i proportional to colony size, giving a per-month likelihood surface
Combined Forecast Model: linear blend of Model 1 (environmental suitability) and Model 2 (GMM likelihood) at ratio 1:beta (beta=1), both rescaled to [0,1]
Coefficient of variation (Vs = sigma/Xbar) as the precision metric for the stochastic K-means/GMM output
Convolutional Neural Network (Model 3) in PyTorch for image classification: 2 conv + 2 pooling + 2 FC layers, input 3x96x96, sigmoid output, 200 epochs, AUC 0.93
Improved TF-IDF text-mining model (Model 4): per-word positive score P_w(Pos) as a Lab-Status-weighted mean, and per-note score P_n(Pos) using a log(term-frequency)-weighted average of word scores
Nonlinear (power/exponential) fusion of CNN image score and text score: ln P(Pos) = ln m * {k*[P_im]^C1*[P_n]^C2 + b}, fitted on training data
Regularized Logistic Regression (Problem c): inputs from Problems a and b, 2 features expanded to 28 via degree-6 polynomial feature mapping, L2 penalty lambda=0.6
Pseudo-labelling / semi-supervised CNN (Model 3*, Problem d): label only high-confidence unlabeled predictions (P>0.9 or P<0.1), retrain, AUC up to 0.96

Subjective weighted-scoring model (Problem d): daily score accrual to a 100-point threshold from 5 weighted factors (environmental suitability, seasonal NCC, major events, GDP, reporting stability) to derive report frequency

Quadratic mixed-effects regression with longitudinal data (Problem e): y_ij = b0+b1*t+b2*t^2 + random effects per site, estimated by MLE, modeling bee and hornet populations under control to detect eradication
key_techniques: Decomposing 'spread' into a spatial axis (where is suitable) and a temporal axis (when/where are clusters), then fusing them — a clean problem decomposition that maps directly onto two well-known methods
Tying model structure to domain biology: number of K-means centers and GMM weights are driven by the documented hornet colony cycle (active Apr-Oct, peak Jul/Aug), not chosen arbitrarily
Principled handling of extreme class imbalance: data augmentation (horizontal/vertical flips to triple positives + a few downloaded images) plus undersampling negatives to a balanced 258/258 set

Turning categorical Lab Status into numeric targets: Positive=1, Negative=0, Unverified=0.5, Unprocessed=P/(P+N), then solving a 2x2 system to recover the implied true positive/negative counts (P=1189, N=3251)
A custom 'improved tf-idf': scoring each word by a Lab-Status-weighted average and using ln(c_w) weighting at the note level to damp rare-word noise (mean shifts 0.301->0.326, validating the log)

Recognizing distributions are non-comparable in shape and therefore choosing a nonlinear (power-law exponent) fusion of image and text scores rather than a naive linear weight; fitted C1≈-39.3 vs C2≈-4.4 quantifies that image dominates text ~9x
Polynomial feature mapping (2->28 features) to give logistic regression nonlinear decision boundaries on only two inputs
Confidence-thresholded pseudo-labelling (only |P-0.5|>0.4) to safely exploit the large pool of unverified/unprocessed reports for Problem d

Operationalizing 'evidence of eradication' as a concrete numeric criterion: a time window (t in [6.83, 7.46]) where hornet pop <=1 and bee pop >=240, derived from extrema of the fitted population curves
Sensitivity analysis on the two free hyperparameters that actually matter (combination weight beta and regularization lambda), shown via boxplot/bar chart, to argue robustness
why_it_won: "It is a judge-pleaser because it answers all five parts with a distinct, named, defensible method for each, yet weaves them into one coherent pipeline — the a+b models feed c, c's CNN feeds d's pseudo-labelling, and a is modified for e. That 'every later part reuses earlier parts' architecture signals genuine modeling maturity rather than five disconnected mini-papers. It is unusually strong on the data side of a 'data' problem: it confronts the brutal 14-vs-2000 imbalance head-on with explicit augmentation/undersampling, exploits all three data modalities (geo-coords, notes, images) instead of just one, and invents a custom Lab-Status-weighted tf-idf to extract signal from reporter notes that most teams ignored. Quantitative self-validation is everywhere — AUC numbers with before/after deltas, accuracy 0.99, coefficient of variation <0.1, and sensitivity sweeps — giving judges concrete evidence of correctness. Domain grounding (colony cycle driving cluster counts, 16 ecology-literature-backed suitability factors with citations) shows the team read the biology, not just the spreadsheet. Finally Problem (e) converts a vague qualitative question ('what is evidence of eradication') into a falsifiable numeric threshold, which is exactly the kind of crisp, decision-ready answer judges reward."
section_highlights: "Strong, scannable structure: a 5-bullet Summary that states the method and a headline result per sub-problem (Vs<0.1, AUC +1.08%, >99% accuracy, AUC->0.96 +2.13%, eradication window), so a judge knows the whole story in 30 seconds. Explicit Assumptions-and-Justifications and Notation sections. Each model gets its own numbered subsection with a Mermaid/flowchart of the pipeline, the governing equations, a 'Results of Model X' paragraph, and a figure. Heavy, purposeful visualization: scree plots, 2D/contour suitability maps, cluster-center scatter plots, word clouds for positive vs negative notes, ROC curves, and population-fit line charts. The CNN architecture is drawn in TikZ. Sensitivity Analysis and a Strengths/Weaknesses Model Evaluation section close the body. A genuinely actionable Memorandum to WSDA translates every model into operational advice (where to trap, when to investigate, how to coach the public to take better photos/notes, how to target queens vs nests). 16 real references from the ecology and ML literature anchor the modeling choices."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Tiny positive class (14 real positives) means the CNN's reported AUC 0.93-0.96 rests on heavily augmented/downloaded images and a 177-sample test set — the headline accuracy is fragile and likely optimistic; downloaded images also risk distribution mismatch with the public-report photos

Several quantitative claims look inflated or numerically inconsistent: the summary says AUC increased 'to 0.96 by 2.13%' while the body says the combination raised AUC '0.94 by 1.08%' and pseudo-labelling '0.96 by 1.08%' — the deltas don't reconcile and percentage-point vs percent is muddled

The nonlinear fusion exponents (C1=-39.3, C2=-4.4) are extreme and fitted on a handful of points; this is almost certainly overfit and not generalizable, and large negative exponents make the formula numerically unstable near zero

The Problem (d) report-frequency model is openly subjective: weights on the five factors are assigned by judgment, the 100-point threshold and '17 points/day' are arbitrary, and the worked example uses assumed numbers — the authors flag this as a weakness

Problem (e)'s eradication conclusion (t in [6.83,7.46], hornets<=1, bees>=240) is produced on hypothetical/illustrative population data, not observed control-program data, so the precise extrema are demonstrative rather than empirically established

Logistic regression in Problem (c) is trained only on reports that have both a positive/negative label and an image — a small, selected subset — so the '0.99 accuracy' generalizes poorly to the unverified/unprocessed majority it is meant to triage
Some assumptions are strong and lightly justified (environmental suitability constant year-to-year; ethnic influence Gaussian over distance; off-season reports simply ignored)

Spatial autocorrelation and reporting bias (reports cluster where people are, not where hornets are) are not corrected for, which can confound both the GMM density and the GDP-weighted frequency model
reusable_patterns: Spatial x temporal decomposition: split a 'spread/diffusion' prediction into a static suitability layer (factor analysis/PCA on environmental covariates) and a dynamic clustering layer (K-means + GMM per time step), then blend with one tunable weight and sensitivity-test that weight

PCA/factor-analysis environmental-suitability index as a reusable recipe: collect literature-backed covariates -> z-score -> keep PCs covering ~85-90% variance -> variance-weighted composite -> affine-rescale to an interpretable score

Multimodal fusion template for data problems: build a separate model per modality (CNN for images, tf-idf for text), inspect the shapes of their output distributions, and pick linear vs nonlinear fusion based on whether the distributions are comparable; report AUC before/after fusion
Imbalance toolkit: augment the minority class (flips/rotations + curated external images) AND undersample the majority to balanced classes; always state the resulting counts and train/test split
Encoding ambiguous categorical labels as probabilities (Positive=1, Negative=0, Unknown=0.5, Unprocessed=base rate) and back-solving a small linear system to estimate latent true class counts

Confidence-thresholded pseudo-labelling to exploit unlabeled data: only adopt predictions with |p-0.5| above a margin, retrain, and report the accuracy/AUC lift as evidence the update mechanism works — a ready-made answer to any 'how do you update the model with new data' sub-problem
Polynomial feature mapping + L2-regularized logistic regression as a compact, interpretable nonlinear classifier when you have very few input features

Turning a vague qualitative sub-question into a falsifiable numeric criterion by fitting population curves and reading off extrema / threshold-crossing windows — strong pattern for 'what counts as evidence of X' questions

Mixed-effects (fixed + random) quadratic regression on longitudinal data to separate population-wide effect from site-to-site variability, estimated via MLE, for any 'population change under intervention' modeling

Document structure pattern: a per-subproblem bulleted Summary with one headline metric each; flowchart + equations + 'Results' + figure per model; sensitivity analysis on only the genuinely free hyperparameters; and a decision-maker Memorandum that converts every model output into concrete operational actions
